title: Module Check: TypeScript Strict Mode and Schema-First Design

questions.question: Name three categories of bugs that `strict: true` in tsconfig catches that a non-strict config would silently allow.
questions.answer: strictNullChecks (null/undefined access), noImplicitAny (untyped parameters), strictPropertyInitialization (class fields possibly undefined). Together they eliminate the most common runtime crashes from unchecked assumptions.

questions.question: Explain the schema=type pattern. What is the role of each layer and why does it prevent schema drift?
questions.answer: The database schema is the single source of truth. Drizzle infers TS types from it ($inferInsert/$inferSelect). Zod validation schemas are derived from those types. Each layer rebuilds from the one below — change the DB schema and the TS type and validator update automatically, preventing the three from diverging.

questions.question: What does `isolatedModules: true` enforce and why does it matter for Vite?
questions.answer: It requires every file to be independently transpilable — no type-only re-exports without `export type`. Vite uses esbuild for single-file transpilation (no full type-check), so files that rely on cross-file type information would silently produce wrong JS without this flag.

questions.question: Which Drizzle utility infers the TypeScript type for a table's INSERT payload?
questions.explanation: $inferInsert infers the shape of data required to insert a row (omits generated/default fields as optional). $inferSelect infers what you get back from a SELECT. InferModel is the older Drizzle API; z.infer is Zod, not Drizzle.

questions.question: In Meridian's stack, what is the correct order of validation layers for an incoming API request?

options.A: DB query → Zod parse → Auth check → Route handler
options.B: Auth check → Zod parse → Route handler → DB query
options.C: Zod parse → Auth check → Route handler → DB query
options.D: Route handler → Auth check → Zod parse → DB query

questions.explanation: Authentication must come first (reject unauthenticated requests before doing any work). Then Zod parses and validates the body. Then the route handler runs business logic. DB queries happen last, after all validation passes. Parsing before auth wastes CPU on bad requests.
